series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/3] cxl/core/region: move pmem region driver logic into region_pmem.c
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the logic for managing persistent memory (pmem) regions from the overloaded region.c file into a new, dedicated region_pmem.c file. This separation makes it clear that this code only applies to pmem regions and helps to declutter the original file. No functional changes are made as part of this refactoring effort.

review_comments.author: Alison Schofield
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch always adds region_pmem.o regardless of CONFIG_CXL_PMEM, and questioned whether the intention is to conditionally compile it based on this configuration option.
review_comments.raw_body:
This always adds region_pmem.o regardless of CONFIG_CXL_PMEM.
Are you heading towards conditionally compiling, where region_pmem.o is
omitted if !CONFIG_CXL_PMEM? 

Similar question for region_dax.o

--Alison

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges reviewer's concern about adding more Kconfig cruft, but doesn't commit to a fix; instead suggests that the current approach might be acceptable despite potential bloat for some deployments.
review_comments.raw_body:
I hadn't considered going that far, but it doesn't seem unreasonable?

It just seemed natural to break out the code this way, but I don't know
if adding more Kconfig cruft is worth it just to save some .o's and a
smaller amount of code.

The recent pmem/nvdimm Kconfig change does kind of show that we might
have some bloat for some deployments which might not care about pmem -
for example.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Jonathan Cameron
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the cxl_pmem_region_release function is missing from region_pmem.c and requested it be added to properly release pmem regions

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron suggested that having region.o and region_pmem.o on one line in the Makefile makes it clearer that they are either both included or neither is, but noted that this would require more changes if the CONFIG_* definitions were later split up.

Reviewer noted that the lifetime management of cxl_pmem_region is more complex than desired, but did not provide specific technical details or suggestions for improvement.

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron noted that removing a blank line from the code could be unnecessary, as there was already one present in the original file and questioned its removal.

The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a pmem region is deleted, and requested that the code be updated to properly clean up resources when a pmem region is removed.

Reviewer Jonathan Cameron expressed concern that the lifetime management of cxl_pmem_region is overly complex, but noted it's not a problem for this specific patch and couldn't immediately suggest improvements.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 6/6] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposed patch to make memory.high tier-aware, but raised concerns about incorrect heuristics and potential OOM issues.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified incorrect heuristics in the proposed patch that could drive swap usage and invert LRU. Suggested revising the logic to manage top-tier memory for noisy-neighbor issues.

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that demotion to lower tier under cgroup memory pressure could still trigger OOM, and asked if this is an issue to address.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/24/26 8:53 PM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 04:21:06PM +0530, Donet Tom wrote:
>> IIUC The intent of this patch is to partition cgroup memory such that
>> 0 \u2192 toptier_high is backed by higher-tier memory, and
>> toptier_high \u2192 max is backed by lower-tier memory.
>>
>> Based on this:
>>
>> 1.If top-tier usage exceeds toptier_high, pages should be
>>   demoted to the lower tier.
>>
>> 2. If lower-tier usage exceeds (max - toptier_high), pages
>>   should be swapped out.
>>
> This is not accurate and an incorrect heuristic.
>
> Transiently, lower-tier usage may exceed (max - toptier_high) for any
> number of reasons which should not be used as signal for pushing swap.
>
> driving swap usage is a function of (usage > memory.high) without regard
> for toptier / lowtier.
>
>> 3. If total memory usage exceeds max, demotion should be
>>   avoided and reclaim should directly swap out pages.
>>
> This is also incorrect, as it would drive agingin inversions.
> Demotion is a natural extension of the LRU infrastructure:
>
> toptier active -> toptier inactive -> lowtier inactive -> swap
>
> if you do (toptier inactive -> swap) you have inverted the LRU.


Thanks, Gregory, for the clarification.

One remaining concern is that under cgroup memory pressure,
demotion to the lower tier can still happen. Since demotion
does not uncharge the memcg, this could still trigger OOM.

Is this an issue we should address?


>
> As far as I know, from testing, we retain all the existing behavior - we
> are just managing a limited resource (top tier memory) to manage the
> noisy-neighbor issue.  So...
>
>
>> Should we also handle cases (2) and (3) in this patch?
> No, I don't think we should
>
> ~Gregory
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] nfs: fix utimensat() for atime with delegated timestamps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS client where delegated timestamps don't properly update the ctime when only the atime is changed using utimensat(). The problem occurs because there's no mtime update to trigger the delegated attribute update. To fix this, the client now issues an RPC to update the ctime alongside the atime.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] nfs: update inode ctime after removexattr operation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the inode ctime after a removexattr operation in NFSv4.2 to ensure that the cached timestamp is refreshed. The problem it solves is that the stat() system call does not trigger a GETATTR request when delegated timestamps are used, resulting in an outdated ctime value. To fix this, the patch adds a trailing GETATTR request to the removexattr operation, similar to how setxattr operations update the inode's timestamp. This change ensures that the inode's ctime is accurately updated after removing extended attributes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the NFS delegated attribute handling, specifically fixing problems with utimensat() and removexattr operations on NFSv4.2 file systems. The patches update inode timestamps and ctime accordingly to ensure consistency and correctness.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/9] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation from the io_uring interface. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, decoupling request fetching from the send operation. This simplifies the control flow and reduces unnecessary parameter passing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the io-uring header copying logic in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver by moving it into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(). This makes the code more clear and centralized, allowing for better error handling and rate-limited logging. The function takes three arguments: the user-space ring buffer address, the header to be copied, and its size. It uses the copy_to_user() function to perform the actual copying and returns an error if it fails. The patch modifies several functions in the dev_uring.c file to use this new function instead of duplicating the copying logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 3/9] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module to make it more clear and centralized. It introduces a new function, copy_header_from_ring(), which handles the actual copying of data from user space to kernel space. This change improves error handling and rate-limited logging. The existing code is updated to use this new function instead of directly calling copy_from_user().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 4/9] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when kernel-managed buffer rings are added. The patch adds a new enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types, and modifies two inline functions `copy_header_to_ring` and `copy_header_from_ring` to use this enum type. This change will make the code more maintainable and easier to understand.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 5/9] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state()` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making it more modular and easier to maintain. The existing code is simplified by removing duplicated functionality and replacing it with calls to this new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 6/9] fuse: support buffer copying for kernel addresses
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer copying in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, specifically allowing it to work directly on kernel addresses rather than requiring page extraction and kmapping. This is a preparatory step for supporting kernel-managed ring buffers in fuse-over-io-uring. The change involves modifying several functions within the FUSE codebase to handle both user-space and kernel-space buffer copying. The patch also includes some minor cleanups and warnings to ensure correct behavior when working with kernel addresses.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 7/9] fuse: add io-uring kernel-managed buffer ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for a kernel-managed buffer ring to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring interface. The buffer ring allows for more efficient communication between the server and the kernel by eliminating the need for pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses, and also reduces memory usage per queue. The feature is enabled on a per-queue basis and requires preregistration of a kernel-managed buffer ring and a memory region to hold the headers. This patch has two benefits: it eliminates overhead in server-kernel interactions and reduces memory needed for buffers per queue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 8/9] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy data transfer over io-uring for the FUSE file system. It allows privileged servers to preregister a kernel-managed buffer ring, a registered memory region, and a sparse buffer corresponding to the queue depth. This enables the elimination of memory copies between the kernel and userspace for read/write operations, resulting in improved performance. Benchmarks show approximately 20-25% increases in throughput for certain workloads. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 9/9] docs: fuse: add io-uring bufring and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer rings and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how to set up kernel-managed buffer rings, which reduce memory allocation requirements and eliminate overheads associated with user page pinning and translation. It also describes the high-level process of using these buffer rings in FUSE operations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer ring and zero-copy capabilities to the FUSE file system over io-uring, reducing memory overhead and enabling direct kernel-to-userspace operations. The changes eliminate the need for pinning and unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses, allowing for incremental buffer consumption and non-overlapping regions of a buffer to be used simultaneously across multiple requests. Zero-copy functionality is only available for privileged servers and allows the server to directly operate on the client's underlying pages, resulting in approximately 20-25% increase in throughput for read-heavy operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`. The first is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()` to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will register bvecs directly. The second is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make its name more generic, as it will be used by both the new and old APIs for unregistration. This change is part of a larger effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/5] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). This is done to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will reuse this logic. The new function initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and allocates memory for it. It also sets up various fields in the structure, including the buffer length, direction, and release function. The original function now calls the helper function to perform the initialization, making the code more modular and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to the io_uring context. The function is designed for use in fuse-over-io-uring zero-copy operations and provides a way to register multiple bvecs at once. It takes several parameters, including the io_uring command, the bvec array, and flags for issue and release. The patch also adds an inline function for the case where the feature is not supported. The new function is implemented in the io_uring/rsrc.c file and includes error handling and locking to ensure thread safety.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 4/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to more descriptive names, IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE. The rename is necessary because callers may need the buffer to be both readable and writable. The flags are exported so that other subsystems can use them. The patch updates several files, including io_uring_types.h, io_uring.c, rsrc.c, and rsrc.h, making changes such as adding new enum values, updating BUILD_BUG_ON macros, and modifying code to use the new flag names.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function `io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()` to the io_uring command handlers. The function allows for retrieving the vmapped pointer to the ring's registered memory region and its size, enabling direct access to pre-registered memory for passing data between the kernel and server. This is specifically used by fuse for reading/writing header data. The patch introduces a new function in `io_uring/rsrc.c` and updates the corresponding inline functions in `include/linux/io_uring/cmd.h`. The function takes three parameters: an io_uring command, a pointer to store the number of pages, and issue flags.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series refactors and extends the io_uring registered buffers infrastructure to allow external subsystems to register pre-existing bvec arrays directly and obtain a pointer to the registered memory region, making fuse zero-copy possible.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 0/5] io_uring: extend bvec registration and add mem region lookup
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`. The first is renamed to `io_buffer_register_request()` to prepare for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, which will register bvecs directly. The second is renamed to `io_buffer_unregister()` to make its name more generic, as it will be used by both the new and old APIs for unregistration. This change is part of a larger effort to support kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/7] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues in the list_lru code, specifically in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function. The problem is that this function can return NULL when !skip_empty, which leads to incorrect behavior. The patch removes these NULL checks and ensures that the function always acquires a lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. This patch is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru, which requires this helper function to manage folio objects correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split shrinker's list_lru allocation is not properly synchronized, and suggested making the code more concise.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch's explanation of the deferred split shrinker's call flow is unclear and suggested a more accurate representation by tracing through the function calls from shrink_slab_memcg() to deferred_split_scan().

The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue uses a per-memcg list, but also sets a bit in shrinker_info->unit->map, which is used to determine the shrink_control in shrink_slab_memcg(), potentially leading to confusion or incorrect behavior.

The reviewer noted that the deferred_split_scan() function walks the same memcg->split_queue regardless of node, which they consider suboptimal.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or creating a new function folio_memcg_alloc_deferred to encapsulate the allocation of the list lru, allowing callers outside huge_memory.c to access it without having to use the global variable.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested splitting the huge zero folio handling into its own function, but noted it's out of scope for this series and should be addressed in a future patch.

The reviewer questioned whether there's a potential race condition when the shrinker is freed, causing the deferred_split_lru to be accessed by another thread.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested renaming the 'lru' variable to something more descriptive, citing that single-letter variable names are not conventional

Reviewer Lorenzo questioned why the deferred split queue was switched to list_lru for anonymous pages specifically, indicating a lack of understanding of the change and requesting clarification.

Reviewer Lorenzo expressed concern about a hidden RCU lock in the code and suggested refactoring the function, possibly splitting out folio_ref_freeze() bits into a separate helper function.

Reviewer Lorenzo pointed out a minor issue with variable naming conventions, specifically that single-letter variable names can be confusing and suggested that 'l' is particularly problematic due to its similarity in appearance to the digit 1.

The reviewer suggested refactoring the code to avoid duplicated logic in list_lru_del(), specifically by making it reference _deferred_list explicitly, instead of having it hardcoded in multiple places.

The reviewer questioned the need for holding a lock while calling list_lru_add(), suggesting that using a locked_add_variant would be more intuitive and avoiding the need for irqsave/restore.

Reviewer Lorenzo agrees that the partially mapped logic is correct, as folio_try_get() will only fail to add a reference if the folio has already lost the race.

The reviewer suggests adding an irqsave/restore variant of list_lru_add() to handle the repeating pattern of acquiring and releasing locks in interrupt-safe manner.

Reviewer Lorenzo suggested moving a stat counter increment operation underneath an if statement to prevent it from being incremented when the operation fails.

The reviewer suggests refactoring the large if-branch in deferred_split_folio() into a separate patch for better bisectability and reviewability.

The reviewer expressed appreciation for the patch's code deletion and thanked the submitter, indicating a positive sentiment.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 6/6] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposed patch to make memory.high tier-aware, but raised concerns about incorrect heuristics and potential OOM issues.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified incorrect heuristics in the proposed patch that could drive swap usage and invert LRU. Suggested revising the logic to manage top-tier memory for noisy-neighbor issues.

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that demotion to lower tier under cgroup memory pressure could still trigger OOM, and asked if this is an issue to address.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/24/26 8:53 PM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 04:21:06PM +0530, Donet Tom wrote:
>> IIUC The intent of this patch is to partition cgroup memory such that
>> 0 \u2192 toptier_high is backed by higher-tier memory, and
>> toptier_high \u2192 max is backed by lower-tier memory.
>>
>> Based on this:
>>
>> 1.If top-tier usage exceeds toptier_high, pages should be
>>   demoted to the lower tier.
>>
>> 2. If lower-tier usage exceeds (max - toptier_high), pages
>>   should be swapped out.
>>
> This is not accurate and an incorrect heuristic.
>
> Transiently, lower-tier usage may exceed (max - toptier_high) for any
> number of reasons which should not be used as signal for pushing swap.
>
> driving swap usage is a function of (usage > memory.high) without regard
> for toptier / lowtier.
>
>> 3. If total memory usage exceeds max, demotion should be
>>   avoided and reclaim should directly swap out pages.
>>
> This is also incorrect, as it would drive agingin inversions.
> Demotion is a natural extension of the LRU infrastructure:
>
> toptier active -> toptier inactive -> lowtier inactive -> swap
>
> if you do (toptier inactive -> swap) you have inverted the LRU.


Thanks, Gregory, for the clarification.

One remaining concern is that under cgroup memory pressure,
demotion to the lower tier can still happen. Since demotion
does not uncharge the memcg, this could still trigger OOM.

Is this an issue we should address?


>
> As far as I know, from testing, we retain all the existing behavior - we
> are just managing a limited resource (top tier memory) to manage the
> noisy-neighbor issue.  So...
>
>
>> Should we also handle cases (2) and (3) in this patch?
> No, I don't think we should
>
> ~Gregory
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

version_history.subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a test failure issue in the generic/747 test, which consistently fails on btrfs due to ENOSPC errors during write/delete cycles. The problem arises from statfs overestimating available space at high fill levels, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even when there's room. To handle this, the patch breaks out of the fill loop when full, deletes a file to free space and retry on failure, and redirects dd stderr to preserve errors for debugging purposes. The test is designed to stress garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space accounting.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the patch's ENOSPC handling may cause unexpected issues due to btrfs' statfs implementation overestimating available space, and suggested addressing this underlying filesystem bug for the benefit of users.
review_comments.raw_body:
f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue of ENOSPC during write/delete cycles could be addressed by improving the calculation of f_bavail, rather than handling it in the test code.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue in patch generic/747 is caused by overestimation of available space due to two factors: btrfs_calc_available_data_space() not handling uneven disk spaces and total_free_data calculation not considering reserved space. He recommended trying a fix for this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
If this is the problem, then I believe we should fix our f_bavail 
calculation.

---

From what I can see, there are several different ways that f_bavail is 
overestimated:

- btrfs_calc_available_data_space()
   That functions goes through each device and try to find unallocated
   space.
   This can not handle uneven space (e.g. 1G + 10G RAID0), and it should
   go through the  per-profile estimation, which handles uneven disks way
   better.

   Normally it shouldn't affect single device, but we can still over-
   estimate by taking the reserved 1M bytes as available.

- total_free_data calculation
   It's just on-disk size minus the on-disk used space, which doesn't
   take things like reserved space into consideration.

   Thus I guess the problem is mostly from this part.

Mind to give this idea a try to fix the long failing g747?

Thanks,
Qu

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the generic/301 test to skip extent count checks on btrfs file systems, as the current threshold was designed for XFS and is not applicable to btrfs due to its different metadata reservation behavior.

\u5728 2026/3/25 07:10, Leo Martins \u5199\u9053:
> The 2/3 fragmentation threshold was designed for XFS's cowextsize
> mechanism and is not applicable to btrfs. After commit 0dc118b3c327
> ("btrfs: be less aggressive with metadata overcommit when we can do
> full flushing"), btrfs commits transactions more frequently during
> writeback, reducing the extent allocator's opportunity to coalesce
> adjacent CoW extents. This causes the extent count to sometimes cross
> the threshold, making the test flaky on btrfs.
> 
> Skip only the extent count assertion for btrfs while keeping the CoW
> write + remount + md5sum data integrity checks, which remain useful.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fix stale errno check in btrfs_encoded_read/write helpers

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: fix stale errno check in btrfs_encoded_read/write helpers

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the btrfs_encoded_read and btrfs_encoded_write helpers, which incorrectly check for an error condition due to a stale errno value. The issue arises when library initialization code sets errno before the atoll() function is called, causing the helper to print usage and exit unnecessarily. To fix this, the patch clears errno before each atoll() call, ensuring that the correct error handling occurs.

> > The test fills a filesystem to 95% then does mixed write/delete cycles,
> > using statfs to decide whether to write or delete. However, statfs
> > f_bavail may overestimate the actual available space. On btrfs, the
> > statfs implementation documents its estimate as "a close approximation"
> > (fs/btrfs/super.c). At high fill levels the discrepancy between what
> > statfs reports and what the filesystem can actually allocate becomes
> > significant, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even though statfs indicated
> > there was room.
> > This is not a filesystem bug. The filesystem correctly rejects the
> > write when it cannot reserve space. The test's purpose is to stress
> > garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space
> > accounting.

f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)
